title: Uniface as a DDE Client
text: Uniface as a DDE Client Uniface provides the following operations to request, send, and execute DDE transactions using the activate ProcScript statement: uDdeRequest —gets data from the server application uDdePoke —sends updated data to the server application (puts data in the application) uDdeExecute —sends commands in the server application uDdeAdvise —establishes an automatic link (warm link) uDdeUnadvise —cancels a warm link The procedures uDdeRequest , uDdeAdvise , uDdePoke , and uDdeExecute normally have a time-out of one second. They will return failure ( $status is -1 ) if the DDE transaction is not completed within that time. For some applications, this time-out is too short. To adjust the time-out value, set the value of the TimeOut setting in the [udde] section of the .ini file. Scripting UDDE Client Requests Setting up a Uniface application as a DDE client requires some ProcScript programming because Uniface does not offer a Paste Link command. All DDE transactions require the client to specify an address containing the following strings: Service —identifies the DDE server application Topic —often identifies a document, a worksheet, or a Uniface component Item —identifies exactly which part of the topic is requested, for example, a cell in a spreadsheet, a bookmark in a word processing application, or a field in Uniface. The operations provided by UDDE do not support parameters, so you need to assign this information to general variables, such as $51, $52, and so on. For example: $50="Calc" $51="system" $53="[Open(%%"c:/wrksht.ods%%")][Select(%%"R4C5%%")][Formula(%%"%%field%%%%%")]" activate "udde".uDdeExecute() if ($status=0) message "Execute succeeded" else message "Execute did not succeed" endif Copy Related Links DDE Links Applications that communiicate via DDE do so using links that can be defined by copying and pasting them to the destination application or programming them using the DDE syntax rules. Variables Variables are placeholders for data whose value can change during the runtime duration of the ProcScript module, component, or application for which they have scope. The scope is determined by where they are declared. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
